section.title: Submission to Governing Authorities
section.sectionRationale: From love within and beyond the church Paul turns to the believer's life under the state. Having just forbidden personal vengeance, he now teaches that God has ordained the governing authority to bear the sword as His servant for good and His avenger against wrongdoing. Submission to lawful authority, the payment of taxes, and the giving of honor are presented as a matter not merely of fear but of conscience before God.
section.thesis: Governing authority is instituted by God as His servant for the public good and His avenger against evil, so believers are to submit for conscience' sake and render what is owed, taxes, revenue, respect, and honor, all under the higher allegiance owed to God alone.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the divine institution of authority and the seriousness of resisting it; the ruler as God's servant for good and avenger of wrath; and the resulting duty to submit in conscience and to pay what is owed.

verses.text: Everyone must submit himself to the governing {{exousia|authorities}}, for there is no authority except that which is from God. The authorities that exist have been appointed by God.
Consequently, whoever resists authority is opposing what God has set in place, and those who do so will bring judgment on themselves.

units.label: The ruler as God's servant
units.note: an agent of retribution, not bearing the sword in vain

verses.text: For rulers are not a terror to good conduct, but to bad. Do you want to be unafraid of the one in authority? Then do what is right, and you will have his approval.

For he is God’s {{diakonos|servant}} for your good. But if you do wrong, be afraid, for he does not carry the {{machaira|sword}} in vain. He is God’s servant, an agent of retribution to the wrongdoer.

units.label: Submit and pay what is owed

verses.text: Therefore it is necessary to submit to authority, not only to avoid punishment, but also as a matter of conscience.
This is also why you pay taxes. For the authorities are God’s {{leitourgos|servants}}, who devote themselves to their work.
Pay everyone what you owe him: taxes to whom taxes are due, revenue to whom revenue is due, respect to whom respect is due, honor to whom honor is due.

exousia.gloss: Authority, the rightful power to govern. Paul says there is no exousia except from God; all legitimate authority is derived, held on loan from the God who is the source of all rule. This both dignifies the office and limits it, for the authority answers to the God who gave it.

diakonos.gloss: A servant, one who ministers on behalf of another. Remarkably, Paul calls the civil ruler God's diakonos, God's servant for your good. The governing authority, whether it knows it or not, serves the purposes of God by restraining evil and promoting order for the common good.

machaira.gloss: A sword, the instrument of judicial force. The ruler does not bear the machaira in vain: God has entrusted to the state the power of the sword to punish wrongdoing. This is the God-appointed answer to personal vengeance, which Paul has just forbidden, placing retribution in the hands of the ordained authority.

leitourgos.gloss: A minister or public servant, a word often used of priestly or official service. Paul calls the tax-collecting authorities God's leitourgoi, God's ministers who devote themselves to their work. The term dignifies civic office as a form of service under God, grounding the duty to pay taxes in God's own ordering of society.

basic.context: Paul has just told the church never to take personal revenge but to leave wrath to God. Now he shows one way God exercises that wrath in this present age: through the governing authority. Having addressed love within the church and toward enemies, he addresses the believer's life under the state. Government, he says, is not a necessary evil but a good ordained by God, His servant for the common good.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this in its place, right after the command to leave vengeance to God. The state is God's appointed means of restraining evil now, while final judgment waits. Paul speaks in general terms of authority as a divine institution; he is not blessing every act of every ruler, and the rest of Scripture makes clear that when the state commands what God forbids, we must obey God rather than men. Here the stress falls on the duty of submission.
basic.originalLanguages: There is no exousia, authority, except from God. The ruler is God's diakonos, His servant for our good, who does not bear the machaira, the sword, in vain. And the tax-gathering officials are God's leitourgoi, ministers who devote themselves to their work. Each word dignifies civil office as service under God.
basic.typology: The ordering of nations under God's rule runs back to His sovereignty over Pharaoh, Cyrus, and Nebuchadnezzar, all of whom served His purposes. The state's sword prefigures and serves the justice that belongs finally to God, and points forward to Christ, the King to whom all authority in heaven and earth has been given.
basic.christ: All earthly authority is derived from the God who has given all authority to the risen Christ. Jesus Himself submitted to Pilate's court, telling him he would have no power unless it were given from above. The Lord who taught us to render to Caesar what is Caesar's and to God what is God's is the true King over every ruler, and every throne will one day bow to Him.
basic.god: God is the source and Lord of all authority. He institutes rulers, works through them for good, and executes a measure of His justice through their office. The passage exalts God's sovereignty over human government, so that submission to lawful authority is ultimately submission to God's own ordering of the world, and the higher allegiance is always to Him.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Peter 2 likewise calls believers to submit to every human institution for the Lord's sake. Titus 3 urges obedience to rulers and readiness for good work. Daniel shows God ruling over kingdoms and setting up rulers. Acts 5 records the apostles insisting they must obey God rather than men.
basic.oneStory: God has always ruled over the nations, raising up and putting down kings to serve His purposes. He ordains government to restrain evil and uphold order until the day of final justice. That day comes with Christ, to whom all authority has been given and before whom every earthly power will bow, when the kingdom of the world becomes the kingdom of our Lord.

context.literary: These seven verses form a compact unit within the ethical section of Romans, flowing directly from the command in 12:19 to leave vengeance to God. The logic is tight: because God repays, and because He has appointed the state as His avenger, believers submit rather than retaliate. The passage moves from principle, verses 1 and 2, to explanation, verses 3 and 4, to application, verses 5 to 7.
context.historical: Paul writes to believers in the capital of the empire, under Nero's early reign, before the later persecutions. Rome kept order, built roads, and administered a rough justice, yet also demanded taxes that stirred unrest. Paul's teaching guided a vulnerable minority to be model citizens where conscience allowed, giving the young church no needless reputation for sedition.
context.canonical: This is the fullest New Testament statement on church and state, read alongside 1 Peter 2, Titus 3, and Jesus' word about Caesar. Together with Acts 5 and Revelation's warnings against a state that demands worship, it forms the balanced biblical teaching: honor and obey lawful authority, yet obey God above all when the two collide.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To teach believers that governing authority is instituted by God for good and for the restraint of evil, so that they submit in conscience, pay what is owed, and give due honor, living as peaceable citizens under God's ordering of society while their highest allegiance remains to God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is prescriptive: it commands submission, the payment of taxes, and the rendering of respect and honor. Yet it describes the God-ordained purpose of the state in general terms, not endorsing every particular action of every government, and it must be read with the whole of Scripture on the limits of obedience.

debates.question: Does this passage command unconditional obedience to every government?

views.label: Submission that is real but not absolute
views.case: The historic reading takes Paul to command genuine submission to lawful authority as God's institution, while the wider witness of Scripture, we must obey God rather than men, sets a limit: when the state commands what God forbids, believers must obey God, though ready to bear the consequences.

views.label: Absolute, unconditional obedience
views.case: A minority have read the commands as allowing no exception, urging obedience to rulers in all things; but this collides with Acts 5, Daniel, and Revelation, and is not the mainstream Christian position.

debates.bearing: The orthodox reading honors the state as God's good ordinance and calls for real submission, while preserving the higher allegiance to God; Paul stresses the duty of submission here without cancelling the rest of Scripture's teaching on its limits.

debates.question: What is the sword the ruler bears in verse 4?

views.label: The judicial power to punish
views.case: The historic reading sees the sword as the state's God-given authority to enforce justice and punish wrongdoing, the appointed alternative to the personal vengeance forbidden in chapter 12.

views.label: Merely a symbol of office
views.case: Some soften it to a symbol of authority alone; but the context of retribution and being afraid points to real coercive power entrusted to the state.

debates.bearing: The sword signifies genuine, God-authorized force to restrain and punish evil, which is why the believer can renounce private revenge and trust God's ordained justice through the magistrate.

study.originalLanguages: Paul grounds the whole passage in one claim: there is no exousia, authority, except from God, so all rule is derived and accountable to Him. He then calls the ruler God's diakonos, His servant for our good, and warns that this servant does not bear the machaira, the sword, in vain, for God has entrusted judicial force to the state as the appointed answer to personal vengeance. Even the tax officials are God's leitourgoi, ministers devoted to their work. Each term dignifies civil office as a form of service under the sovereign God who ordains it.

typology.fulfillment: God's sovereignty over Pharaoh, Cyrus, and Nebuchadnezzar is fulfilled in Christ, to whom all authority in heaven and earth is now given.
The state's God-given sword serves the justice that belongs finally to God and is consummated when Christ returns to judge.
The civil servant of God foreshadows the true and perfect King, Christ, who rules in righteousness over every earthly throne.

study.christ: All authority is derived from the God who has now given all authority in heaven and on earth to the risen Christ. Jesus lived out this teaching, submitting even to Pilate's unjust court, yet telling him plainly that he would have no power at all unless it were given from above. He taught His people to render to Caesar what is Caesar's and to God what is God's, holding the two in right order. The Lord who is King over every ruler will one day gather all thrones under His feet, when the kingdoms of this world become His.
study.god: God is the sovereign source and Lord of all authority. He institutes rulers, works through them for the common good, and executes a measure of His justice through the office of the state, His appointed avenger against wrongdoing in the present age. To submit to lawful authority is therefore to submit to God's own ordering of the world; yet because that authority is derived, it is never absolute, and the believer's highest allegiance is always to the God who gave it.

crossReferences.note: Submit for the Lord's sake to every human institution; honor the emperor, but fear God above all.
Be subject to rulers and authorities, obedient, and ready for every good work.
God changes times and seasons; He removes kings and sets up kings, ruling over the kingdoms of men.
We must obey God rather than men, the limit on obedience when the state commands what God forbids.

study.oneStory: From Pharaoh to Cyrus, God has ruled over the nations, raising up and casting down kings to serve His purposes. He ordains human government to restrain evil and uphold order in the time before the end, His servant and avenger in a fallen world. The story runs toward the day when Christ, to whom all authority has been given, returns to judge in perfect justice, and every earthly throne bows before the King of kings.

quiz.q: Where does governing authority come from, according to Paul?

options.text: From God, who institutes the authorities that exist.
options.note: Paul says there is no authority except that which is from God.

options.text: From the consent of the people alone.
options.note: However rulers arise, Paul roots their authority ultimately in God.

options.text: From the raw power of whoever seizes control.
options.note: Authority is not mere power grabbing; it is instituted by God.

options.text: From nowhere; it is purely a human invention.
options.note: Paul explicitly grounds authority in God's appointment.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe the civil ruler in verse 4?

options.text: As God's servant for our good and an agent of retribution against wrong.
options.note: The ruler is God's diakonos, not bearing the sword in vain.

options.text: As an enemy the church should always resist.
options.note: Paul calls the ruler God's servant for good, not an enemy to resist.

options.text: As a purely neutral figure with no relation to God.
options.note: Far from neutral, the ruler is called God's servant.

options.text: As a private individual with no public duty.
options.note: The ruler holds a God-given public office for the common good.

quiz.q: Why should believers submit, according to verse 5?

options.text: Not only to avoid punishment, but also as a matter of conscience.
options.note: Submission is a matter of conscience before God, not mere fear.

options.text: Only out of fear of being punished.
options.note: Fear of punishment is real, but Paul adds the deeper reason of conscience.

options.text: Only when the ruler happens to be righteous.
options.note: Paul grounds submission in God's institution, not the ruler's private virtue.

options.text: Never; conscience forbids all submission.
options.note: Paul commands submission as a matter of conscience, not against it.

quiz.q: What does Paul say believers owe the authorities in verse 7?

options.text: Taxes, revenue, respect, and honor, each to whom it is due.
options.note: Paul lists the concrete debts of the citizen to the state.

options.text: Nothing at all, since believers are free in Christ.
options.note: Paul explicitly commands paying what is owed.

options.text: Only religious devotion, never taxes.
options.note: Paul names taxes and revenue specifically, not only inward attitudes.

options.text: Complete worship as to God Himself.
options.note: Worship belongs to God alone; the state receives honor and dues, not worship.

quiz.q: How does this passage relate to the higher allegiance owed to God?

options.text: Authority is real but derived from God, so we obey God above all when they conflict.
options.note: The orthodox reading: submit to the state, yet obey God rather than men when commanded to sin.

options.text: It demands unconditional obedience to rulers in absolutely everything.
options.note: Scripture as a whole limits obedience: we must obey God rather than men.

options.text: It teaches that the state may be freely ignored whenever inconvenient.
options.note: Paul commands genuine submission, not casual disregard.

options.text: It says the state has no relation to God at all.
options.note: The state is precisely God's servant, derived from and accountable to Him.

questions: Paul roots all authority in God's appointment. How does seeing government as part of God's ordering of the world shape the way you think and speak about those who govern?
Submission is to flow from conscience, not merely fear of punishment. Where might God be calling you to a more honest, conscientious citizenship?
The ruler is called God's servant for good. How can you pray for and honor those in authority even when you disagree with them?
Paul places the state's sword right after forbidding personal revenge. How does trusting God's ordained justice free you from taking matters into your own hands?
Our highest allegiance is always to God. How do you discern the difference between lawful submission to authority and the point where you must obey God rather than men?

translationNotes.note: Governing authorities renders a phrase for the ruling powers; Paul's point is that all legitimate authority is derived, there is none except from God, which both dignifies and limits every human office.

He does not carry the sword in vain speaks of the state's God-given judicial power to punish wrongdoing, the appointed alternative to the personal vengeance forbidden in 12:19; agent of retribution renders a word for one who executes wrath.
God's servants here renders leitourgoi, a term for public or even priestly ministers, dignifying civic office as service under God and grounding the duty to pay taxes in His ordering of society.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 13:1-7. No footnote markers occur in this passage. No em or en dashes used; punctuation kept to commas, colons, and semicolons. Church and state handled orthodoxly: the state is God's good ordinance calling for real submission, while the higher allegiance to God is preserved, obeying God rather than men when they conflict. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
